title: Outcome of anorexia nervosa: Eating attitudes, personality, and parental bonding
abstract:
Outcome of Anorexia Nervosa: Eating Attitudes,
Personality, and Parental Bonding
Cynthia M. Bulik,1* Patrick F. Sullivan,1 Jennifer L. Fear,2 and Alison Pickering3
1 Virginia Commonwealth University, Department of Psychiatry, Virginia Institute for
Psychiatric and Behavioral Genetics, Richmond, Virginia
2 New Zealand Health Information Service, Ministry of Health,
Wellington, New Zealand
3 University Department of Psychological Medicine, Christchurch School of Medicine,
Christchurch, New Zealand
Accepted 29 November 1999
Abstract: Background: We examined eating attitudes, personality, and parental bonding in
women with a history of anorexia nervosa stratified by degree of recovery in comparison to
randomly selected controls. We were interested in the distinguishing characteristics of re-
covery and of chronic anorexia nervosa. Method: All female new referrals to an eating
disorders service between January 1, 1981 and December 31, 1984 with probable or definite
anorexia nervosa were eligible for inclusion. 86.4% of these women (“cases”) were located
and agreed to participate. The control group was a random community sample. All subjects
were interviewed with a structured diagnostic instrument and completed a battery of psy-
chological inventories including the Eating Disorder Inventory (EDI), the Three-Factor Eating
Questionnaire (TFEQ), the Temperament and Character Inventory (TCI), and the Parental
Bonding Instrument (PBI). Results: We divided the anorexia follow-up sample into full re-
covery (n = 21), partial recovery (n = 34), and chronically ill (n = 15) and compared them to
community controls (n = 98). The chronically ill group was distinguished by a lower desired
body mass index (BMI), higher cognitive restraint on the TFEQ, higher Drive for Thinness and
Bulimia on the EDI, lower maternal and paternal care on the PBI, and high harm avoidance
and low self-directedness on the TCI. The full recovery group scored high on self-directedness
and cooperativeness on the TCI. Conclusions: The domains of personality, character, and
parental bonding differ among categories of recovery in anorexia nervosa. Whether these
differences contribute to recovery or emerge during recovery or lack thereof remains an
unanswered question. © 2000 by John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Int J Eat Disord 28: 139–147, 2000.
Key words: anorexia nervosa; outcome; personality; parental bonding
*Correspondence to: Dr. Bulik, Virginia Commonwealth University, Department of Psychiatry, Virginia Institute
for Psychiatric and Behavioral Genetics, P.O. Box 980126, Richmond, VA 23298-0126. E-mail: cbulik@hsc.
vcu.edu.
Contract grant sponsor: Canterbury Medical Research Foundation.
© 2000 by John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
 INTRODUCTION
Anorexia nervosa is a serious psychiatric disorder associated with substantial morbidity
and mortality (Sullivan, 1995; Sullivan, Bulik, Fear, & Pickering, 1998). Several reviews
have examined the outcome from this often debilitating disorder (Herzog, Keller, &
Lavori, 1988; Hsu, 1980, 1988; Schwartz & Thompson, 1981; Steinhausen & Glanville, 1983;
Steinhausen, Rauss-Mason, & Seidel, 1991; Strober, Freeman, & Morrell, 1997; Swift, 1982).
This is a second in a series of reports from a study designed to assess the outcome of
anorexia nervosa across a number of outcome domains by addressing many of the meth-
odological deficiencies outlined by Hsu (1980). In the initial report, we examined eating
disorder status and psychiatric comorbidity comparing cases and controls (Sullivan et al.,
1998). In the present report, we stratify the anorexia nervosa sample by recovery status
and examine results from the psychological test battery in comparison to community
controls. The study employed a historical observational cohort design in which cases of
anorexia nervosa who were assessed or treated over a decade earlier were located and
followed up and paired with a case-control study.
The present design allowed us to address several pertinent questions. First, we were
able to explore what distinguished women who were fully recovered from women who
recovered partially or who developed a chronic illness. Second, we were able to examine
differences between women who recovered fully and community controls in order to
identify residual differences despite the remission of the eating disorder. Finally, we were
able to examine the distinguishing characteristics of women who continued to suffer from
an eating disorder on average 15 years after their initial diagnosis.
METHODS
Overview
We identified, located, and interviewed—using clear diagnostic definitions and struc-
tured methodology—70 women who were referred for treatment of anorexia nervosa an
average of 12 years earlier (a mean of 15.4 years after onset of anorexia nervosa) in order
to describe their intermediate- to long-term outcome. Then, using a case-control design,
we compared the outcome of these cases with anorexia nervosa to the status of a random
community control group screened for the presence of anorexia nervosa. The study was
reviewed and approved by the local ethical committee and all subjects provided written
informed consent.
Case Definition and Ascertainment
A complete description of the methods for this study can be found in Sullivan et al.
(1998). Potential cases were drawn from the records of the Eating Disorders Service at The
Princess Margaret Hospital in Christchurch, New Zealand. Any woman assessed or
treated by the Eating Disorders Service for the first time from January 1, 1981 to December
31, 1984 was eligible for inclusion. Hospital records were independently reviewed and
abstracted by two medical students under the close supervision of PFS. Following a
consensus conference between the two raters and PFS, potential cases were defined as
anyone who met criteria for definite or probable anorexia nervosa outlined in the 3rd ed.
140
Bulik et al.

Outcome of Anorexia Nervosa: Eating Attitudes,
Personality, and Parental Bonding
Cynthia M. Bulik,1* Patrick F. Sullivan,1 Jennifer L. Fear,2 and Alison Pickering3
1 Virginia Commonwealth University, Department of Psychiatry, Virginia Institute for
Psychiatric and Behavioral Genetics, Richmond, Virginia
2 New Zealand Health Information Service, Ministry of Health,
3 University Department of Psychological Medicine, Christchurch School of Medicine,
Abstract: Background: We examined eating attitudes, personality, and parental bonding in

covery and of chronic anorexia nervosa. Method: All female new referrals to an eating

and cooperativeness on the TCI. Conclusions: The domains of personality, character, and
parental bonding differ among categories of recovery in anorexia nervosa. Whether these
differences contribute to recovery or emerge during recovery or lack thereof remains an
unanswered question. © 2000 by John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Int J Eat Disord 28: 139–147, 2000.
Key words: anorexia nervosa; outcome; personality; parental bonding
*Correspondence to: Dr. Bulik, Virginia Commonwealth University, Department of Psychiatry, Virginia Institute
for Psychiatric and Behavioral Genetics, P.O. Box 980126, Richmond, VA 23298-0126. E-mail: cbulik@hsc.
Contract grant sponsor: Canterbury Medical Research Foundation.
© 2000 by John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
Anorexia nervosa is a serious psychiatric disorder associated with substantial morbidity
and mortality (Sullivan, 1995; Sullivan, Bulik, Fear, & Pickering, 1998). Several reviews
have examined the outcome from this often debilitating disorder (Herzog, Keller, &
Lavori, 1988; Hsu, 1980, 1988; Schwartz & Thompson, 1981; Steinhausen & Glanville, 1983;
Steinhausen, Rauss-Mason, & Seidel, 1991; Strober, Freeman, & Morrell, 1997; Swift, 1982).
This is a second in a series of reports from a study designed to assess the outcome of
anorexia nervosa across a number of outcome domains by addressing many of the meth-
odological deficiencies outlined by Hsu (1980). In the initial report, we examined eating
disorder status and psychiatric comorbidity comparing cases and controls (Sullivan et al.,
1998). In the present report, we stratify the anorexia nervosa sample by recovery status
and examine results from the psychological test battery in comparison to community
controls. The study employed a historical observational cohort design in which cases of
anorexia nervosa who were assessed or treated over a decade earlier were located and
followed up and paired with a case-control study.
The present design allowed us to address several pertinent questions. First, we were
able to explore what distinguished women who were fully recovered from women who
recovered partially or who developed a chronic illness. Second, we were able to examine
differences between women who recovered fully and community controls in order to
identify residual differences despite the remission of the eating disorder. Finally, we were
able to examine the distinguishing characteristics of women who continued to suffer from
an eating disorder on average 15 years after their initial diagnosis.
We identified, located, and interviewed—using clear diagnostic definitions and struc-
tured methodology—70 women who were referred for treatment of anorexia nervosa an
average of 12 years earlier (a mean of 15.4 years after onset of anorexia nervosa) in order
to describe their intermediate- to long-term outcome. Then, using a case-control design,
we compared the outcome of these cases with anorexia nervosa to the status of a random
community control group screened for the presence of anorexia nervosa. The study was
reviewed and approved by the local ethical committee and all subjects provided written
Case Definition and Ascertainment
A complete description of the methods for this study can be found in Sullivan et al.
(1998). Potential cases were drawn from the records of the Eating Disorders Service at The
Princess Margaret Hospital in Christchurch, New Zealand. Any woman assessed or
treated by the Eating Disorders Service for the first time from January 1, 1981 to December
31, 1984 was eligible for inclusion. Hospital records were independently reviewed and
abstracted by two medical students under the close supervision of PFS. Following a
consensus conference between the two raters and PFS, potential cases were defined as
anyone who met criteria for definite or probable anorexia nervosa outlined in the 3rd ed.
(DSM-III; American Psychiatric Association [APA], 1980) and the 3rd Rev. ed (DSM-III-R;
APA, 1987) of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders.
To locate potential cases, we arranged a computerized search of the New Zealand
mortality data base to identify any cases who may have died, followed up addresses in
hospital charts (e.g., for the person, parents, and general practitioner), searched all New
Zealand telephone and electoral roll directories, searched the New Zealand register of
Births, Deaths, and Marriages, and checked the telephone directories and electoral rolls
for the major metropolitan areas in Australia.
Control Definition and Ascertainment
We randomly selected individuals from the 1993 Christchurch electoral rolls. All adults
are required by New Zealand law to register for the electoral roll and 95.5% of the adult
population was in fact registered (Beautrais et al., 1996). These women were matched to
the controls by gender and age banding (i.e., current age 23–45 years). Three controls with
a history of anorexia nervosa or subthreshold anorexic symptoms were excluded, yielding
After cases and controls were contacted and agreed to participate, they were inter-
viewed with the Diagnostic Interview for Genetic Studies (DIGS; Frances, 1994; Nurn-
berger et al., 1994) and were rated on the Global Assessment Functioning scale (GAFS;
Endicott, Spitzer, Fleiss, & Cohen, 1976). Because of the nature of the contact protocol, it
was impossible for the interviewer to be blind to case-control status.
Participants then completed the Eating Disorders Inventory (EDI; Garner, Olmsted, &
Polivy, 1984), the Three-Factor Eating Questionnaire (TFEQ; Stunkard & Messick, 1985),
the Parental Bonding Instrument (PBI; Parker, Tupling, & Brown, 1979), and the Tem-
perament and Character Inventory (TCI; Cloninger, Przybeck, Svrakic, & Wetzel, 1994).
We defined recovery based on status at the time of follow-up and defined our catego-
ries based on the fully recovered and chronically ill endpoints. We included in the chroni-
cally ill group all participants who met criteria for an eating disorder at the time of
interview (anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, or eating disorder not otherwise specified)
(n = 15). Individuals who were fully recovered met the following criteria: no current
eating disorder diagnosis, weight >85% ideal body weight (IBW), and no current binging
and purging (n = 21). All other individuals who did not have a current eating disorder
diagnosis but who reported current binging or purging or maintained a weight <85% IBW
comprised the partially recovered group (n = 34). We chose not to base recovery on scores
on the psychometric instruments so that we could retain those instruments as dependent
We used simple statistics (chi-square test, analysis of variance [ANOVA], and analysis
of covariance [ANCOVA]) to compare the three recovery groups and controls (SAS In-
stitute Inc., 1994). As age differed between cases and controls, it was included as a
covariate in all analyses. All p values are two tailed. Given the small cell size of the
chronically ill group, we chose to limit our analyses to univariate statistics. In order to
correct for multiple comparisons, we adopted a critical value of p < .01.
Case-Control Demographic Comparisons
The participation rate for cases was 86.4% (70/80) and 91.7% (101/111) for controls,
which was not statistically different (p = .23). The mean age of the controls was signifi-
cantly greater than that of the cases. Age was therefore incorporated as a covariate in the
analyses that follow. Cases and controls were otherwise similar in demography.
Eating Disorder-Related Variables
Table 1 presents comparisons across the three recovery groups and controls for eating
and weight-related variables, the EDI, and the TFEQ. The BMI at the time of interview
was significantly lower for all three recovery groups than for controls and the three
groups did not differ from each other. Similarly, there was no difference across the three
recovery groups in terms of lowest past body mass index (BMI) or highest past BMI,
although all three of these groups differed significantly from controls in the expected
direction. Whereas all three recovery groups had lower ideal BMIs than controls, the
chronically ill group desired a significantly lower BMI than either the partially or fully
There was no difference in the age of onset of anorexia across the three groups. The
Means comparisons for eating-related variables
Note: BMI = body mass index; AN = anorexia nervosa; BN = bulimia nervosa; GAFS = Global Assessment of
Functioning Scale; TFEQ = Three-Factor Eating Questionnaire; EDI = Eating Disorders Inventory.
*F and p values presented control for age. Means and standard deviations are unadjusted for age.
control group reported a significantly older age of first diet than any of the recovery
groups. A lifetime history of bulimia nervosa was present in all individuals in the fully
recovered group, in 80% of the chronically ill group, and in 24% of the partially recovered
group. The chronically ill women had significantly lower GAFS scores than all others and
the partially recovered women had lower GAFS than controls.
On the TFEQ cognitive restraint scale, the chronically ill women scored significantly
higher than the partially and fully recovered women and controls. In addition, controls
reported lower cognitive restraint than both the fully and partially recovered women.
There were no differences across the four groups on disinhibition or hunger. On the EDI,
the chronically ill women scored higher on Drive for Thinness and Bulimia than the other
three groups. All three anorexia groups scored significantly higher than controls on Per-
fectionism and the partially recovered women scored lower than the chronically ill on this
Personality and Family Variables
For the temperament measures, the chronically ill group had significantly higher harm
avoidance than either the fully recovered group or controls (Table 2). On the character
scales, the fully recovered group and the controls had significantly higher self-
directedness and cooperativeness than either the partially recovered or the chronically ill
groups. On the PBI, the chronically ill group reported significantly lower maternal care
than all other groups, and significantly lower paternal care than the fully recovered and
There are several strengths to this study that allow an informative profile of the out-
come of anorexia nervosa. First, we studied individuals at the point of first referral,
avoiding oversampling individuals with chronic anorexia nervosa and thereby address-
Means comparisons for personality and family variables
Note: TCI = Temperament and Character Inventory; PBI = Parental Bonding Instrument.
*F and p values are corrected for age. M and SDs are unadjusted for age.
ing the question of an individual’s prognosis at the point of first referral. Second, as has
been advocated (Herzog et al., 1988; Hsu, 1980; Steinhausen et al., 1991), we paid careful
attention to a number of procedural details including case ascertainment, tracing, inter-
viewing, and the use of structured diagnostic interviews. Third, by including a commu-
nity control group, we were able to determine how the fully recovered group differed
from women with no history of anorexia nervosa.
Consistent with our prior report (Sullivan et al., 1998), women with anorexia nervosa,
regardless of their recovery status, maintained a BMI that was significantly below that of
the control population, even when controlling for age. In attempting to distinguish among
the three anorexia groups, there was little difference in terms of the course of their eating
disorder. They had all begun dieting at roughly the same age, reported similar lowest and
highest past BMIs, and the age at onset of anorexia was indistinguishable. The control
women who did diet reported a much later age of onset of their first diet, suggesting that
preventive efforts designed to delay or eliminate the onset of dieting in young girls may
The distribution of lifetime bulimia nervosa across the three anorexia groups was
unequal. Every member of the fully recovered group and 80% of the chronically ill group
reported lifetime bulimia nervosa in comparison to 24% of the partially recovered group.
Thus, it is unclear whether “conversion” to bulimia nervosa constitutes a good or poor
prognostic sign. In our attempts to determine differences in the nature of bulimia or the
course of development of bulimia, we found no differences between the fully recovered
and chronically ill groups (data not shown). Perhaps, the emergence of bulimia assumes
different prognostic significance depending on other characteristics of the individual. For
example, the emergence of bulimia in an individual with high self-directedness may
presage positive treatment response and recovery, whereas bulimia in an individual with
low self-directedness may be a ticket to chronicity.
On the eating-related scales (TFEQ and EDI), all three anorexia groups continued to
show significantly more cognitive restraint than the controls. Cognitive restraint is the one
eating-related measure that differed significantly between fully recovered women and
controls, suggesting that restraint continues to dominate their attitudes toward eating
despite recovery from the eating disorder. This also suggests that the maintenance of a
low BMI may be driven by continued restraint rather than reflecting some genetic pre-
disposition to thinness or a metabolic adaptation to having had anorexia nervosa.
The Perfectionism scale of the EDI provides another window to examine how women
with a history of anorexia nervosa, even when fully recovered, differ from controls. The
control women had significantly lower Perfectionism scores than all three anorexia
groups. Prior studies have also observed persistent perfectionism after recovery from
anorexia (Srinivasagam et al., 1995). Perfectionism could emerge subsequent to the diag-
nosis of anorexia nervosa. However, when a trait remains after complete remission of a
disorder, it may reflect a trait phenomenon that represents a risk factor for the develop-
What we term perfectionism in this study has been observed for decades under varying
guises. This characteristic has been studied as perfectionism (Bruch, 1978), as “obsessiod”
characteristics (which persisted after weight restoration; Pillay & Crisp, 1977), a “modern
obsessive-compulsive syndrome” (Rothenberg, 1986), and many more. The relation be-
tween this type of character structure and the development of anorexia has been sum-
marized best by Strober (1980), who remarked that these premorbid personality charac-
teristics remain fairly stable after weight restoration and have a facilitative role in exac-
erbating the more general obsessive and compulsive traits seen during the acute phase of
the illness. Further confirming the relation between anorexia and this trait, a controlled
family study has revealed that anorexia and obsessive-compulsive personality traits may
share familial risk factors (Lilenfeld et al., 1998).
We also observed marked differences in temperament, character, and parental bonding
across our recovery groups. The TCI is divided into four temperament measures (novelty
seeking, harm avoidance, reward dependence, and persistence) and three character di-
mensions (self-directedness, cooperativeness, and self-transcendence). The chronically ill
women reported significantly higher harm avoidance, lower self-directedness, and lower
cooperativeness than either controls or fully recovered women. Although the harm avoid-
ance scale is affected by the presence of depression (Cloninger et al., 1994), the three
recovery groups did not differ in terms of the prevalence of lifetime major depression or
depression at the time of follow-up (data not shown). Thus, the higher harm avoidance
that characterizes the chronically ill group does not merely reflect a greater prevalence of
depression. This is consistent with a follow-up study conducted by Casper (1990) who
found high harm avoidance among recovered anorexics in comparison to healthy con-
Of particular note, the fully recovered group scored significantly higher than the par-
tially recovered or chronically ill anorexics and was comparable to controls on the self-
directedness and cooperativeness scales. These two scales have received considerable
attention as low scores on these dimensions reliably predict the presence of a personality
disorder (Svrakic, Whitehead, Przybeck, & Cloninger, 1993). We have argued that low
self-directedness may capture the essence of having a personality disorder (Bulik, Sulli-
van, Carter, & Joyce, 1995). In studies of bulimia nervosa, we have found that high
self-directedness is associated with good outcome in bulimia nervosa (Bulik, Sullivan,
Carter, McIntosh, & Joyce, 1998) and predicts rapid response to cognitive-behavioral
therapy in bulimia nervosa (Bulik, Sullivan, Carter, McIntosh, & Joyce, 1999). Thus, al-
though it is theoretically possible that high self-directedness and cooperativeness emerge
from having a positive experience with recovery from anorexia nervosa, it is perhaps
more likely that the premorbid presence of these characteristics enhances one’s ability to
Finally, results from the PBI indicate significantly lower maternal and paternal care
scores in women who are chronically ill. Two possible interpretations arise from this
finding. First, the long-standing presence of an eating disorder may adversely affect the
family system and may bias an individual’s perception of her parents. Second, the absence
of adequate maternal and paternal care may truly contribute to the chronicity of eating
disorders. This effect may be direct or indirect. It is possible that the individuals in the
chronically ill group arise from what Mullen, Martin, Anderson, Romans, and Herbison
(1993) referred to as a “matrix of disadvantage.” This is manifested in the chronicity of
their eating disorders, their elevated comorbidity, and their personality profiles that re-
flect a belief that their destiny is not within their own control.
The present study allowed us to determine characteristics of women who recover fully
from anorexia nervosa as well as characteristics of those who develop a chronic course.
Regardless of recovery state, women with a history of anorexia nervosa maintain a BMI
that is markedly lower than that of controls. This may be related to persistent cognitive
restraint, which was observed in all three recovery groups. Likewise, women with a
history of anorexia nervosa, even if fully recovered, score significantly higher on Perfec-
tionism, suggesting that perfectionism may represent a trait phenomenon that could
potentially increase the risk of developing an eating disorder. Full recovery from anorexia
nervosa is associated with the character traits of self-directedness and cooperativeness,
whereas chronic illness is associated with high harm avoidance and low maternal and
PFS received a research training fellowship from the Health Research Council. We are greatly
indebted to Professor Peter Joyce and Drs. Janice McKenzie, J. Elisabeth Wells, Kim McFadden, and
Ingrid Jolley for their assistance. Above all, we thank the participants.
